abstract: This article examines how major social media platforms—Meta, YouTube, X, and TikTok—discursively construct, justify, and normalize ongoing change in their features, policies, and governance. Through qualitative analysis of 301 public-facing communications (blog posts, newsroom announcements, policy updates) from April 2022 to January 2025, the authors develop the concept of 'changecraft' to describe the strategic discursive practices platforms use to manage transformation. They argue that platforms render infrastructural shifts visibly aesthetic, reframe ideological pivots as continuity, and deploy small patchworked updates that cumulatively reorient platform logics while masking power consolidation and deepening user dependence.
key_claims: Platform change is not only a technical or social process but a deeply discursive and political one that warrants its own conceptual vocabulary.
Platforms exhibit 'discursive flexibility,' strategically narrating change to appear neutral, responsive, and innovative while consolidating power and increasing user dependency.

Changecraft operates through three primary strategies: making infrastructural shifts visible (inverting typical infrastructure invisibility), reframing ideological shifts as continuity, and using 'patchworking' to enact incremental but cumulatively significant restructuring.

Rationalizations of change rely on recurring tropes—creator benevolence, democratization/participatory rhetoric, and connectivity—that legitimize platform interventions as natural, beneficial, or community-driven.
Unlike prior accounts emphasizing concealment, platforms actively manage and stage change discursively rather than simply hiding it, partly in response to regulatory pressures like Germany's NetzDG.
methods: Qualitative content analysis of 301 platform-authored documents from Meta (n=88), TikTok (n=70), X (n=70), and YouTube (n=58), collected April 2022–January 2025.
Documents drawn from official newsroom/blog channels (about.fb.com/news, creators.facebook.com, newsroom.tiktok.com, blog.x.com, blog.youtube).
Iterative inductive thematic analysis (following Braun and Clarke) conducted in ATLAS.ti, beginning with five randomly sampled documents per platform and refining categories across rounds.
Selective application of discourse analysis techniques (Wood and Kroger) attending to framing, positioning, and what is left unspoken.

Data reduction to retain only platform updates, company communications, user-directed messages, and management statements; analytic memoing and team discussion to develop themes of material change, ideological change, and rationalization.
findings: Material changes are foregrounded through visible interface redesigns, screenshots, and feature demos (e.g., Meta's AI Backgrounds for Messenger, YouTube Shorts on TV) that emphasize 'how' rather than 'why,' creating an aesthetic of progress.
Material updates often conceal deeper monetization and control shifts, as with Meta Reels ranking/payout changes and YouTube's likeness-management tools framed as creator protection.

Ideological changes are presented as recalibration rather than rupture, using phrases like 'updating our approach,' 'long-standing efforts,' and 'original intention' (e.g., Meta's pivot away from third-party fact-checking, X's post-acquisition transparency messaging).
Creator benevolence framing legitimizes infrastructural and monetization changes by casting platforms as supportive mentors (e.g., TikTok Creator Studio, YouTube Hype, Instagram Best Practices).
Democratization rhetoric ('for fans, by fans'; 'collaborative approach to teen supervision') simulates participatory governance without ceding decision-making power.
Connectivity discourse ties feature changes (Views metric, stickers, subscriptions) to community-building while embedding monetization, surveillance, and engagement-maximization goals.
contributions: Introduces 'changecraft' as a novel analytic concept for studying platform transformation as a discursive practice.
Provides a shared vocabulary—infrastructural visibility, ideological recalibration, and patchworking—for platform studies scholars to interrogate how change is communicated.
Extends prior work on platform discourse (Gillespie, Plantin, Helmond) by shifting analytic focus from concealment to strategic discursive control of change.
Offers a comparative empirical mapping of change discourse across four major platforms, identifying recurring rationalization tropes (benevolence, democratization, connectivity).
Bridges platform studies with infrastructure studies by showing how platforms invert the typical invisibility of infrastructure, making change aesthetically visible as a governance tactic.
framing: The paper situates itself within platform studies and critical discourse scholarship on social media governance, drawing on van Dijck, Gillespie, Plantin, Helmond, and Poell et al. to argue that existing case-by-case analyses of platform policy or feature changes lack a shared conceptual language. It positions itself in dialogue with work on platform transience (Barrett and Kreiss), platformization and infrastructuralization (Helmond et al.), and platform paternalism (Petre et al., Cotter), while engaging infrastructure studies (Star, Larkin) and public-relations theory (Bakir et al.) to theorize change as discursively managed.
